ch.p: Styrian is written in Cyrillic. The standard orthography preserves the historically grounded spelling distinction between [st и :t y] /ɪ~ɨ/ and [st і :t i] /i/, but writes [st е :t e] for all vowels descended from Proto-Slavic *e, *ě (yat), and the strong front jer alike, reflecting the completed merger of these sounds in the modern phonological system.

sec.p: ¹ [st г :t h] is pronounced as a voiced glottal or pharyngeal fricative /ɦ/, distinct from [st х :t ħ] /x/. Written [st г :t h] in all positions.

[st ц :t c] /ts/ and [st ч :t č] /tʃ/ are synchronic phonemes of Styrian. The only šт-forms that arise from earlier consonant change are those from the independent *kt > *xt > št path (e.g. [st ноштӑ :t noštă] "night" from *noktь); these are explicitly distinguished from words retaining an original affricate such as [st чернӑ :t černă] or [st цветӑ :t cvetă]. Loans and proper names may be spelled with [st ц :t c] and [st ч :t č] directly.

The language has no productive palatalized consonant series. Older velar alternations survive only as a small fossilized morphophonological pattern before front-vowel inflection in a closed, lexically specified subclass, plus a few lexical residues elsewhere (see §Fossilized Velar Mutation).

r.c: written [st г :t h], pronounced as glottal fricative; distinct from [st х :t ħ]
also covers former *ě (yat), now fully merged
reflex of the older hard high vowel layer (old y/[st ӑ] in strong position)
reflex of the older front high vowel layer (old i/[st ь] in strong position)
writes post-vocalic [st йі :t ji] as a single letter: [st тої :t toji], [st мої :t moji], [st сеї :t seji]; after consonants plain [st і :t i] is written instead
consonantal glide; used in medial and final position; word-initially in я and ю
plain lateral; no productive palatal partner
velar fricative; distinct from [st г :t h]
dental affricate; retained from Proto-Slavic; used in native words, loans, and proper names
postalveolar affricate; retained from Proto-Slavic; distinct from the [st шт :t št] cluster that derives from *kt
reflex of all weak jers; word-final support vowel; [ɐ] stressed, [ə] unstressed, [ɤ̞] in careful or eastern speech
iotated /u/; represents initial /j/ + /u/; retained from native *ju- vocabulary
iotated /a/; represents initial /j/ + /a/; retained from native *ja- vocabulary

list.item: [st ё :t io] is not part of standard Styrian orthography; the /jo/ sequence, found only in loanwords, is written [st йо :t jo]
[st ь] is not used in native spelling; the soft jer has fully merged into [st ӑ :t ă]

Inherited consonant + [st в :t v] + vowel clusters are normally repaired by vocalizing [st в :t v] to glide-like [st у :t u]: thus [st туой :t tuoj] /tvoj/, [st суой :t suoj] /svoj/, [st доуа :t doua] /dva/

sec.p: The consonantal glide /j/ is written [st й :t j] in medial and final position. When [st й :t j] is followed by [st і :t i] after another vowel, the standard orthography writes [st ї :t ji] instead of the digraph [st йі :t ji]: thus [st тої :t toji], [st мої :t moji], [st суої :t suoji]. After consonants there is no separate glide in this environment, so plain [st і :t i] is written. Outside this post-vocalic pattern, word-final [st и :t y] is always the vowel /ɪ~ɨ/ and is never consonantal in this orthography:

The iotated letters [st я :t ya] and [st ю :t yu] handle initial /j/ + vowel sequences in native vocabulary. Only initial /j/ in a small closed class of grammatical items has been lost historically (see §Diachronic Development, Period I).
The modern stressed vowel inventory is:

Unstressed vowels are centralized or shortened, and many have been lost entirely after the initial syllable. [st ӑ :t ă] occurs in both stressed and unstressed syllables: unstressed [ə] is common in inherited weak-jer positions and final support vowels, while stressed [ɐ] appears in a limited inherited and analogically extended lexical stratum. Its underlying value is best given as /ɐ/, with a broader phonetic range [ɐ ~ ə ~ ɤ̞] depending on stress and speech style. In the standard language, stressed [st ӑ :t ă] is normally [ɐ]; unstressed [st ӑ :t ă] is normally [ə]; a somewhat backer realization [ɤ̞] may occur in careful speech or some eastern varieties, but this is not the core standard value.

Stress normally falls on the first syllable of the lexical word. Aspectual verbal prefixes do not carry stress, so the stress remains on the first syllable of the verbal base: [st пісат :t pisat] písat, but [st напісат :t napisat] napísat, not *[st написат :t napisat] nápisat.
Clitics and other unstressed function words (articles, conjunctions, object clitics) do not carry stress of their own and lean phonologically on the following or preceding lexical word.

The preferred syllable shapes are CV, CVC, CCV, and CCVC. Native words avoid three-consonant onsets, long final clusters, and inherited sequences like -[st мл :t ml]j-, -[st рдл :t rdl]-, -[st вск :t vsk]-. The language is far more "Romance-like" in its surface shape than most Slavic languages.

list.item: Final schwa: the surviving weak jer [st ӑ :t ă] /ə/ provides a syllabic nucleus after a word-final consonant cluster: *dobrъ > [w dobrə], *moglъ > [st моглӑ :t mohlă]. This replaces the epenthetic а/е repair found in other Slavic branches.
Simplification: one or more consonants are deleted: *zemlja > [w zema] (the lj cluster simplifies; final -а is original)
Metathesis: a consonant shifts position (e.g. liquid metathesis in *korlь > [st кралӑ :t krală])

Affricate–liquid epenthesis: the onset clusters [st цл :t cl] /tsl/, [st цр :t cr] /tsr/, [st чл :t čl] /tʃl/, and [st чр :t čr] /tʃr/ are prohibited. Affricates ([st ц :t c] /ts/ and [st ч :t č] /tʃ/) each contain an internal stop-plus-fricative sequence; a directly following liquid constitutes a third onset element, violating the ban on three-consonant onsets. The repair is insertion or retention of /e/ between the affricate and the liquid — the same quality as the general liquid-support vowel. A historically weak jer before the liquid in such a cluster is treated as phonotactically strong and vocalizes to [st е :t e] rather than deleting: *čьlovekъ > [st чeловекӑ :t čelovekă] "person" (contrast West Slavic člověk, where the initial jer deletes). For loanwords without a historical jer, synchronic [st е :t e] is inserted by epenthesis.

sec.p: Voiced/voiceless assimilation applies across morpheme boundaries in natural speech but is not always reflected in spelling.

*kt development. The cluster *kt developed as *kt > *xt > [st шт :t št] through velar weakening and coronal assimilation, independently of the Bulgarian/OCS palatalization path. This yields [st шт :t št] in words such as [st ноштӑ :t noštă] (from *noktь). Affricates [st ц :t c] and [st ч :t č] that derive from other Proto-Slavic sources are unaffected and are written directly.
